item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll downvote begrudgingly, mind you, because I hate Prometheus Lab's idea of using incredible sciencemagic for the most dumb, mundane crap you can ever imagine in a serious way. I would love it if it had some goofy aspect or it was aware of its own stupidity but its not. It's played in earnest and I hate that. Other than that it's a nice skip, but I just can't ignore that.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Oh my god, I genuinely hadn't seen this post up until now, but I'm glad my attempt at a Series I-style throwback to short and sweet worked for as many people as it did.

it's honestly nice to have some SCPs that don't take 3 minutes to read and another 5 minutes going through the comments figuring them out. this was short and sweet and effective.

also, this is my first time getting any page here to a milestone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Horrifically late, but I've fixed this.

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
I've gone for the double-whammy here and edited in a cross-link to both the Prometheus Labs Hub and another paraeconomics skip by GreenWolf, namely SCP-2308.

If possible, I'll try and work in a crosslink to SCP-2791, because a) it's also paraeconomical and b) an underrated skip that I absolutely adore, but that last one might be a stretch.

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show proposed edit
- Title for hiding text

To link to other prometheus content, I propose the following edit:

SCP-2630 is the designation for a computing complex built by Prometheus Paraeconomics in 1968 known as NOTUS (Necromantic Ontologically and Thaumically Unified Supercomputer).

to

SCP-2630 is the designation for a computing complex built by Prometheus Paraeconomics in 1968 known as NOTUS (Necromantic Ontologically and Thaumically Unified Supercomputer).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I didn't like the article up until the little whiplash at the end. I thought it was roughly standard and never really caught my attention until the end. That turned my downvote to an upvote really fast.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fifteen of these intestines are bovine rumens, while the rest are human large intestines.

the state of the bovine rumens encodes the price of sixteen S&P 500 stocks approximately nineteen hours in the future, with each rumen corresponding to a different stock.

Do I misunderstand something here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to write about para-finance… I am both pleased and saddened that you beat me to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's just a machine that converts mechanical forces into market forces.

HA!

I didn't even think about that, actually.

i am ashamed. pun skills why have you deserted me. ._.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see what's so special about this. It's just a machine that converts mechanical forces into market forces.

upvoted

In 4 days.

What.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am going to try and indicate the rooms of my house as volumes now :D

This is my livingvolume.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A short SCP that has that delightful PL flavor of using powerful magic and paratech to anomalously achieve some everyday end. In that sense I guess this is on par with a Rube Goldberg in terms of complexity of steps to results. I can dig it.

+1 AE.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a fucking haruspex robot and they use it for predicting stocks. God, I love skips that involve complex magical methods for achieving mundane results.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spoilers for various loose ends now available here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a feeling that this will make it to the big time.

Call it a gut feeling.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's like if someone left an Ophite SCP on the stove for a while and some of it boiled away. That's not a bad thing on its own, and neither is the article, but I don't think it's the right way to make a haruspicy machine. You execute the whiplash, but don't have anywhere to go with it, because it's a very straightforward article.

No vote. I think there's a quality article here, but I fall just short of feeling it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First point: it can just mean a general space when it's used like in "dining area": which typically implies your diners are eating in more than two spatial dimensions. x3

Second point: I'll give it a second skimthrough today just to see if I can tighten up the prose a little bit. Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, we've had a spate of good posts today! I love the idea here - it's fantastic - and you've conveyed all the key details with pleasing efficiency.

Just two things I'd say:

  • "SCP-2630 occupies an approximate area of 3.5 x 5.0 x 3.0 meters (WxLxH)": an area with three dimensions? Whuh-oh!!! Don't you mean volume? ;)
  • I think you can tighten up the writing. There aren't any sentences which are especially weak compared to the others, so I won't pick out particular examples, but I feel that you have a couple of superfluous or slightly off-tone words here and there. Jigging around with those would get you even closer to the famous punchiness of ophite's work. Ninja-edit: not just the individual words, but the phrasing too: e.g. in the above sentence, "occupies an approximate area of…" should really be "occupies an area of approximately…" - after all, it's not the area itself that's "approximate", it's the measurement of the area.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DING DING DING DING

BWOOOOOOP BWOOOOOOP

WE HAVE A WINNER

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: HarusCo is a proud partner of Prometheus Laboratories and it's subsidiaries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: CSS cut and ideas stolen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…a haruspicy-bot.

Prometheus Laboratories: Beyond the cutting edge since 1892.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously? I'd love to see a PL scientist just very casually bring this up in a board meeting like it's not at all horrifying. +1 10/10 would disembowel again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was more or less what I expected when I started reading the article, yeah. Another reason might be that they made it so that the only documentation of it that can exist is the most recent version of their user manual (so there's no misinformation around), so the Foundation would just put that verbatim in the slot - though that concept might work better for a different GOI. Or perhaps Prometheus might just have gotten some contract work analysing this object the Foundation was stuck with, and so the current entry for the object in the Foundation database is from their technical report until the Foundation gets around to writing a formal article. Or hell, maybe some of the background knowledge is from a defunct Prometheus Tech Support Hotline run by a snarky AI that's annoyed that it has to keep the line going when the labs have gone defunct.

(writers are encouraged to steal these ideas because now I want to read all of them but don't have the time right now to write any of them)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
an SCP document which was actually written by Prometheus (which would certainly be interesting)

Like a Foundation that fully (and openly) integrated Prometheus Labs into their R&D?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also agree that SCP article CSS should be left as normal unless it is an element of the article itself; at the moment on the site, fancy CSS gets used to identify a few GOI Format pages, or (as with Third Law stuff in general) to unify a canon. When I saw the formatting, I was confused about whose perspective it was from, thinking that it might somehow be an SCP document which was actually written by Prometheus (which would certainly be interesting), and then… it wasn't. This is still documentation from the perspective of the Foundation, and I feel like the style of the page should reflect that.

I'm not typically one to criticise odd stylistic choices, but they need to be in service of the content. I don't feel that's the case here - all it did was briefly confuse me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I don't think these stylistic thingies go well on mainlist articles outside of meta SCPs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am somewhat confused by the presence of the Third Law stylistic on an SCP article. Perhaps it's your choice, but I do not think it is relevant to the article (beyond the PL reference).1

Beyond that, it is lovely to see another para-economics SCP. This one feels more magic-based.

Footnotes
1. By that logic, Third Law should apply to every PL article on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Compu- Comp… Computer?
+1

item.title: --psat setanta-- necromancy stock when
item.content:encoded:
Another draft I sat on and posted only now, at the urging of GreenWolf. This seems to be a pattern.

This was semi-written to emulate the kind of semantic whiplash ophite used to such effect in SCP-1193. With any luck I'll have gotten a fraction of the potency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved it in chat and I love it here.

Take your dammed +1 you genius.
